stories.headline: Nasdaq sinks ~4% in worst day since April 2025 as Broadcom's soft AI outlook triggers a chip-stock rout
stories.summary: The Nasdaq fell about 4% to 25,709, the S&P 500 dropped 2.6% and the Dow lost roughly 695 points as a semiconductor selloff wiped out hundreds of billions in market value. The slide was triggered by Broadcom, which reported 143% AI-revenue growth but guided next-quarter AI revenue below expectations, sinking its stock about 13% and dragging Nvidia, AMD, Micron and Arm lower. Hot jobs data and rising Treasury yields amplified the move.

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as a single guidance miss erasing roughly $1 trillion, exposing how stretched and concentrated the AI-driven market has become.
perspectives.summary: Left-leaning coverage led on Broadcom's outlook falling short of investors' AI-fueled expectations, arguing the violent reaction shows how dependent the market has grown on a handful of richly valued AI names.

sources.title: Broadcom Outlook Falls Short of Investor Expectations for AI Revenue Growth

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as Broadcom's strong-but-not-blowout AI outlook puncturing lofty expectations and detonating a broad semiconductor selloff.
perspectives.summary: Market coverage detailed the Nasdaq's roughly 4% drop — its worst day since April 2025 — and tied it to Broadcom's guidance, with chipmakers Nvidia, AMD, Micron and Arm falling and a hot jobs report and higher yields adding pressure.

sources.title: Stock Market Today (June 5, 2026): Nasdaq falls 4% as semiconductor slide wipes $1T from markets
Nasdaq falls 4% and suffers worst day since April 2025 as traders flee chip stocks

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as a healthy reality check for a priced-for-perfection chip sector, not a break in the AI thesis.
perspectives.summary: Market commentary noted that even 143% AI revenue growth was not enough for a sector priced for perfection, casting the selloff as a long-overdue correction in overheated semiconductor valuations rather than a thesis-breaking event.

sources.title: Stock Market Today, June 4: Broadcom Shares Plunge After AI Outlook Misses High Investor Expectations
Broadcom Sinks 14% on Soft AI Chip Outlook Despite Earnings Beat, Dragging Down AMD and Intel

stories.headline: Putin vows to bolster air defenses after Ukrainian drones strike St. Petersburg during economic forum
stories.summary: At the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum, President Putin acknowledged damage from deep Ukrainian drone strikes on the city and said Russia will strengthen its air defenses, while dismissing European pressure. President Trump said June 5 he believes the war is close to being settled and backed direct talks, even as Russia continued strikes on Kyiv amid Ukraine's air-defense shortfall.

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as Russia escalating the aerial war even as Trump claims a settlement is near.
perspectives.summary: AP-sourced coverage reported Putin's vow to bolster air defenses after Ukrainian drones struck St. Petersburg, alongside Trump's claim that the war is close to being resolved and continued Russian strikes on Kyiv.

sources.title: Putin says Russia will bolster air defenses in response to Ukrainian drone attacks
Putin says Russia will bolster air defenses in response to Ukrainian drone attacks

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as Ukrainian deep strikes embarrassing Putin on his home turf during his showcase economic forum.
perspectives.summary: Western coverage highlighted that Ukrainian drones hit St. Petersburg just hours before the opening of 'Putin's Davos,' underscoring Kyiv's reach deep into Russia even as its own air defenses are stretched.

sources.title: Ukrainian drones strike St. Petersburg, hours before 'Putin's Davos' opens

perspectives.keyFraming: US congressional action framed as pushing against Trump's softer line by backing sanctions and aid.
perspectives.summary: Coverage of Capitol Hill noted the House voted to back fresh Russia sanctions and Ukraine aid, a move presented as a bipartisan rebuke of the administration's more conciliatory posture toward Moscow.

sources.title: US House Backs Russia Sanctions, Ukraine Aid, in Latest Blow to Trump

stories.headline: Senate blocks FISA Section 702 surveillance extension; warrantless spy powers set to expire June 12
stories.summary: The Senate voted 47-52 against advancing a reauthorization of FISA Section 702 warrantless surveillance powers, which now expire June 12 absent action. Seven Republicans joined Democrats in opposition; Sen. John Fetterman was the lone Democrat in favor. Democrats tied their opposition to Trump's appointment of Bill Pulte as acting Director of National Intelligence.

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as Democrats withholding votes to keep sweeping surveillance powers out of the hands of an inexperienced, partisan acting DNI.
perspectives.summary: Left-leaning coverage emphasized that Democrats blocked the extension to protest Trump's appointment of Bill Pulte as acting intelligence director, casting it as a check on handing warrantless spying tools to a political loyalist.

sources.title: Senate Democrats block extension of FISA 702 spy powers to protest Pulte as DNI

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as a bipartisan coalition stalling 702 reauthorization, putting a key intelligence program days from going dark over privacy and personnel concerns.
perspectives.summary: Wire and Capitol Hill coverage reported the 47-52 procedural failure, with seven Republicans joining Democrats, and noted the program lapses June 12 unless the Senate acts amid a fight over the acting DNI.

sources.title: Senate fails to extend FISA surveillance program as deadline nears, with 7 Republicans joining Democrats
FISA reauthorization stalls in early-morning Senate vote

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as Democratic obstruction over a personnel grievance threatening a vital national-security surveillance tool.
perspectives.summary: Conservative coverage cast the block as Democrats jeopardizing a key foreign-surveillance authority to register objections to Trump's acting DNI, warning the lapse weakens counterterrorism capabilities.

sources.title: Senate Dems block reauthorization of foreign surveillance law over objections to Trump's acting DNI

stories.headline: IAEA chief Grossi says US and Iran appear 'pretty close' to a nuclear framework agreement
stories.summary: IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi said June 5 that Washington and Tehran appear close to a framework to manage Iran's nuclear file following the April ceasefire, while cautioning that the enriched-uranium stockpile question and inspector access remain unresolved political issues. He spoke after an IAEA Board of Governors meeting, noting Iran still controls inspectors' access to its sites.

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as diplomacy gaining ground, with a framework that could lock in the post-war ceasefire.
perspectives.summary: Regional and South Asian coverage led on Grossi's assessment that a US-Iran framework is near, presenting it as a stabilizing step that could consolidate the ceasefire reached after the spring conflict.

sources.title: IAEA chief says US-Iran talks close to nuclear framework agreement
IAEA chief: US-Iran nuclear framework talks near deal

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed around the caveat that any deal is hollow while Iran still controls inspector access to its nuclear sites.
perspectives.summary: Iran-focused coverage stressed Grossi's warning that the enriched-uranium stockpile and limited verification remain unresolved, with Tehran still controlling whether inspectors can reach key sites.

sources.title: Grossi says Tehran still controls IAEA access to nuclear sites

stories.headline: Drone strikes kill dozens of civilians across Sudan's Kordofan in a fresh escalation of the war
stories.summary: A June 3 drone strike on civilian vehicles heading to a market in North Kordofan killed at least 18 people, including two children; separate strikes across West and North Kordofan killed scores more. The attacks deepen a pattern in which the UN says drones have become the leading cause of civilian deaths in Sudan's war, even as the conflict draws scant Western front-page coverage.

perspectives.keyFraming: Sudanese outlets foreground named victims and displacement sites, framing drone warfare as deliberate targeting of fleeing civilians — a granular human toll US wires omit.
perspectives.summary: Sudanese coverage detailed the North Kordofan market-road strike that killed at least 18, including children, and tallied 60-plus deaths across two days of drone attacks in Darfur and Kordofan, emphasizing the civilian victims and displacement. Limited Western coverage accompanies these reports.

sources.title: Drone Strike Kills 18 Civilians Traveling to Market in North Kordofan
Two days of drone strikes leave 60+ dead across Sudan's Darfur and Kordofan regions
